data.description:

  • The share price has moved 2.5% over the last week and 8.5% over the last month, with a 31.0% return over the past year, set against a 12.6% decline year to date and a 106.7% and 135.2% return over 3 and 5 years respectively.

This context is important for considering whether the current share price already reflects that long term potential or still leaves room for mispricing.

Approach 1: American Express Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model asks a simple question: after covering the required return for shareholders, how much extra value does American Express generate on its equity base, and what is that worth per share today?

For American Express, book value is $48.80 per share and the stable book value used in the model is $58.40 per share, based on future book value estimates from 8 analysts. The model applies a stable EPS of $21.00 per share, drawn from return on equity estimates from 11 analysts, against a cost of equity of $4.86 per share. That gap leads to an excess return of $16.15 per share, supported by an average return on equity of 35.96%.

Combining these inputs, the Excess Returns model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $387.57 per share. Compared with the recent share price around $325.76, this points to an implied discount of roughly 15.9%, indicating that the shares are currently assessed as undervalued on this basis.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: American Express Price vs Earnings

P/E is a useful way to value a profitable company because it ties the share price directly to the earnings that each share generates. In simple terms, it tells you how many dollars investors are currently willing to pay for one dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on what the market expects for future growth and how risky those earnings look. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while lower growth or higher risk usually line up with a lower multiple.

American Express currently trades at about 20.88x earnings. That sits above the Consumer Finance industry average P/E of 9.08x, and slightly below the peer group average of 22.06x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for American Express is 18.35x, which reflects the multiple that would be expected given its earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market value and risk factors.

The Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry because it adjusts for company specific characteristics rather than assuming one size fits all. Compared with this Fair Ratio of 18.35x, the current P/E of 20.88x suggests the shares are trading at a premium on this metric.

data.title: TFI International Expands Upper Midwest Warehousing To Deepen Integrated Services
data.description:
• TA Dedicated, a TFI International (TSX:TFII) company, has acquired Triangle Warehouse.
  • The deal adds roughly 900,000 square feet of warehousing space in the Upper Midwest to TFI's network.
  • Triangle Warehouse also brings additional fleet units that expand TA Dedicated's distribution capabilities.

TFI International, through TA Dedicated, operates in truckload, less than truckload, and logistics services, with this move adding more warehousing and distribution depth. For shippers, the extra space and fleet capacity can support multi use supply chain setups that combine storage, transportation, and value added services in one network.

For investors watching TSX:TFII, this transaction highlights the company’s focus on growing its presence in the Upper Midwest and broadening its service mix. The added footprint could open the door to new customer relationships and cross selling opportunities as TA Dedicated integrates Triangle Warehouse into its broader network.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for TFI International by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on TFI International.

2 things going right for TFI International that this headline doesn't cover.

This acquisition pushes TFI International further into integrated warehousing and transportation, an area where competitors like C.H. Robinson, XPO and Knight-Swift are also investing. Adding roughly 900,000 square feet of space and about 150 power units gives TA Dedicated more control over freight flows in the Upper Midwest, which can matter for service reliability and contract wins. For shippers, a single partner that can provide storage, temperature controlled capacity and national distribution can simplify vendor relationships and potentially increase switching costs in TFI International’s favor.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The TFI International Narrative

  • The Triangle Warehouse deal aligns with the narrative of using operational efficiency and a broader service offering to support margins, because more owned warehousing can help TFI International optimize routes and load planning across its network.
  • It also tests the narrative that acquisition opportunities are limited, since each new deal adds integration work and execution risk that could slow progress on other efficiency initiatives if not managed tightly.
  • The increased focus on cold storage and complex supply chain services is only lightly reflected in the existing narrative, which mostly centers on freight volumes, U.S. LTL execution and digital efficiency rather than warehousing-led solutions.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for TFI International to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Integration risk if Triangle Warehouse’s operations, systems and culture do not align smoothly with TA Dedicated’s existing network.
  • ⚠️ Higher capital intensity from more owned warehouse space and fleet could pressure returns if freight demand or storage utilization are weaker than expected.
  • 🎁 Additional warehousing and cold storage capacity can make TFI International more relevant for customers that want end to end supply chain solutions with a single provider.
  • 🎁 The Upper Midwest expansion may support cross selling across truckload, less than truckload and logistics, which could deepen existing customer relationships over time.

What To Watch Going Forward

Investors may want to watch how quickly TFI International fills the added 900,000 square feet, how Triangle Warehouse’s customer base responds to access to a broader national network, and whether management shares any detail on returns from recent acquisitions. Updates around utilization of the 150 additional power units, service levels for existing accounts and any new contract wins tied directly to the expanded footprint will help indicate whether this deal is strengthening TFI International’s competitive position versus peers.

• Sherwin-Williams (NYSE:SHW) reports a record year with strong fourth quarter results.
  • The company outlines plans to expand its store footprint in 2025 with up to 100 new store openings.
  • Management also proposes a significant dividend increase for shareholders.

Sherwin-Williams, a major player in paints and coatings, supplies products to professional contractors, industrial customers, and DIY homeowners. The record year and solid fourth quarter performance highlight how the business is positioned across both construction and remodeling activity. For investors, the mix of professional and consumer demand can be an important factor when comparing NYSE:SHW with other building products and home improvement names.

The plan to add up to 100 stores in 2025 and the proposal for a higher dividend indicate how management is thinking about capital allocation and growth priorities. These moves can affect everything from future cash flow needs to how income-focused investors view the stock. Readers may want to track how store openings and the dividend proposal progress through 2025 company updates.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Sherwin-Williams by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Sherwin-Williams.

Is Sherwin-Williams's dividend sustainable? Check out what every dividend investor needs to know in our dividend analysis.

The proposed 10.5% dividend increase, on top of a record year and solid fourth quarter, sends a clear signal about how Sherwin-Williams views the resilience of its cash flows. Management is pairing higher cash returns with plans for 80 to 100 new stores in 2025, so investors are seeing both reinvestment in the business and income returned through dividends and buybacks. For income-focused holders, the key questions are whether earnings and free cash flow can comfortably cover a larger dividend and ongoing repurchases, especially given earlier commentary about soft demand in some end markets and conservative profit guidance. The long history of annual dividend increases suggests a pattern of prioritising shareholder returns, but it also raises the bar for maintaining or growing the payout through cycles. Compared with other coatings and materials names such as PPG Industries and RPM International, this combination of expansion spending and higher dividends underlines Sherwin-Williams' confidence in its business model, while still leaving execution risk if housing and DIY demand remain patchy.

How This Fits Into The Sherwin-Williams Narrative

  • The record year, store expansion and proposed dividend increase line up with the narrative of Sherwin-Williams using growth investments and cost discipline to strengthen market share and earnings power.
  • Guidance that reflects soft demand and operating inefficiencies could make it harder to deliver the level of margin improvement and earnings growth some narrative assumptions are built on.
  • The specific size and timing of the dividend increase, together with ongoing buybacks, is not fully captured in the narrative and may influence how investors weigh reinvestment versus cash returns.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Sherwin-Williams to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged at least one company risk, including concerns about Sherwin-Williams having a high level of debt, which can matter when dividends and buybacks rise.
  • ⚠️ Soft demand in key end markets and conservative profit guidance leave some risk that higher dividends and store openings stretch cash flows if conditions stay weak.
  • 🎁 Earnings are forecast to grow 8.28% per year, which, if realised, would support both the higher payout and continued investment in growth projects.
  • 🎁 A long track record of dividend increases, combined with ongoing buybacks, signals a consistent commitment to returning capital to shareholders over time.

What To Watch Going Forward

Investors may want to track how the higher dividend interacts with Sherwin-Williams' cash generation over the next few quarters, especially as the 80 to 100 planned store openings roll through the income statement and cash flow statement. Watch for commentary on payout ratios, free cash flow after dividends and buybacks, and any updates to sales and earnings guidance as demand conditions evolve. Comparisons with peers such as PPG Industries and RPM International on dividend growth, leverage and reinvestment can also help frame whether Sherwin-Williams' capital allocation mix looks balanced.

data.title: Here's Why We Think Sunlite Recycling Industries (NSE:SUNLITE) Is Well Worth Watching

It's common for many investors, especially those who are inexperienced, to buy shares in companies with a good story even if these companies are loss-making. Loss-making companies are always racing against time to reach financial sustainability, so investors in these companies may be taking on more risk than they should.

Despite being in the age of tech-stock blue-sky investing, many investors still adopt a more traditional strategy; buying shares in profitable companies like Sunlite Recycling Industries (NSE:SUNLITE). Now this is not to say that the company presents the best investment opportunity around, but profitability is a key component to success in business.

How Fast Is Sunlite Recycling Industries Growing?

Generally, companies experiencing growth in earnings per share (EPS) should see similar trends in share price. That makes EPS growth an attractive quality for any company. It certainly is nice to see that Sunlite Recycling Industries has managed to grow EPS by 17% per year over three years. If the company can sustain that sort of growth, we'd expect shareholders to come away satisfied.

Careful consideration of revenue growth and earnings before interest and taxation (EBIT) margins can help inform a view on the sustainability of the recent profit growth. EBIT margins for Sunlite Recycling Industries remained fairly unchanged over the last year, however the company should be pleased to report its revenue growth for the period of 57% to ₹19b. That's progress.

To give you an idea, the value of insiders' holdings in the business are valued at ₹3.6b at the current share price. So there's plenty there to keep them focused!

With EPS growth rates like that, it's hardly surprising to see company higher-ups place confidence in the company through continuing to hold a significant investment. The growth and insider confidence is looked upon well and so it's worthwhile to investigate further with a view to discern the stock's true value. data.title: Arbeit-Times' (TSE:2341) Performance Is Even Better Than Its Earnings Suggest
data.description:
Arbeit-Times Co., Ltd. (TSE:2341) just reported healthy earnings but the stock price didn't move much. We think that investors have missed some encouraging factors underlying the profit figures.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

Examining Cashflow Against Arbeit-Times' Earnings

As finance nerds would already know, the accrual ratio from cashflow is a key measure for assessing how well a company's free cash flow (FCF) matches its profit. The accrual ratio subtracts the FCF from the profit for a given period, and divides the result by the average operating assets of the company over that time. The ratio shows us how much a company's profit exceeds its FCF.

Therefore, it's actually considered a good thing when a company has a negative accrual ratio, but a bad thing if its accrual ratio is positive. While it's not a problem to have a positive accrual ratio, indicating a certain level of non-cash profits, a high accrual ratio is arguably a bad thing, because it indicates paper profits are not matched by cash flow. To quote a 2014 paper by Lewellen and Resutek, "firms with higher accruals tend to be less profitable in the future".

Over the twelve months to February 2026, Arbeit-Times recorded an accrual ratio of -0.13. Therefore, its statutory earnings were quite a lot less than its free cashflow. In fact, it had free cash flow of JP¥311m in the last year, which was a lot more than its statutory profit of JP¥189.0m. Notably, Arbeit-Times had negative free cash flow last year, so the JP¥311m it produced this year was a welcome improvement. Importantly, we note an unusual tax situation, which we discuss below, has impacted the accruals ratio.

Note: we always recommend investors check balance sheet strength. Click here to be taken to our balance sheet analysis of Arbeit-Times.

An Unusual Tax Situation

In addition to the notable accrual ratio, we can see that Arbeit-Times received a tax benefit of JP¥19m. This is meaningful because companies usually pay tax rather than receive tax benefits. The receipt of a tax benefit is obviously a good thing, on its own. And since it previously lost money, it may well simply indicate the realisation of past tax losses. However, our data indicates that tax benefits can temporarily boost statutory profit in the year it is booked, but subsequently profit may fall back. In the likely event the tax benefit is not repeated, we'd expect to see its statutory profit levels drop, at least in the absence of strong growth. So while we think it's great to receive a tax benefit, it does tend to imply an increased risk that the statutory profit overstates the sustainable earnings power of the business.

Our Take On Arbeit-Times' Profit Performance

In conclusion, Arbeit-Times has strong cashflow relative to earnings, which indicates good quality earnings, but the tax benefit means its profit wasn't as sustainable as we'd like to see. Considering the aforementioned, we think that Arbeit-Times' profits are probably a reasonable reflection of its underlying profitability; although we'd be confident in that conclusion if we saw a cleaner set of results. In light of this, if you'd like to do more analysis on the company, it's vital to be informed of the risks involved. For example, we've discovered 2 warning signs that you should run your eye over to get a better picture of Arbeit-Times.

Our examination of Arbeit-Times has focussed on certain factors that can make its earnings look better than they are. But there are plenty of other ways to inform your opinion of a company. For example, many people consider a high return on equity as an indication of favorable business economics, while others like to 'follow the money' and search out stocks that insiders are buying. data.title: Robertet (ENXTPA:RBT) Margin Stability And 12.2% EPS Growth Support Bullish Narratives
data.description:
Robertet (ENXTPA:RBT) opened FY 2025 with first half revenue of €446.3 million and basic EPS of €28.55, while trailing 12 month revenue stood at €839.4 million and EPS at €46.79, setting a clear earnings benchmark against a current share price of €814. Looking back, revenue has moved from €414.6 million in the first half of 2024 to €393.1 million in the second half and then to €446.3 million in the first half of 2025, with basic EPS stepping through €24.71, €18.35 and €28.55 over the same periods, giving investors a concrete view on how margins are feeding through to the bottom line.

See our full analysis for Robertet.

With the latest numbers in place, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the prevailing Robertet narratives and where the data challenges the current stories around growth and profitability.

TTM earnings growth at 12.2%

  • Over the last 12 months, net income reached €96.9 million on €839.4 million of revenue, with earnings up 12.2% and a net margin of 11.5% compared with 11.4% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for the bullish view is how this earnings growth lines up with the idea of a steady, high quality business, yet still leaves questions:
    • Bulls point to earnings growth of 12.2% against a five year annualised rate of 10.7% as support for a consistent earnings profile rather than a one off spike.
    • At the same time, revenue growth expectations of about 5.2% a year suggest a more measured top line pace, so the bullish case leans heavily on margin stability and reported earnings quality rather than rapid expansion.

11.5% margin and €58.5m half year profit

  • In the first half of FY 2025, Robertet recorded €446.3 million of revenue and €58.5 million of net income, which feeds into the 11.5% trailing net profit margin cited for the last 12 months.
  • What is interesting for bullish investors is how this profitability profile supports confidence in earnings, while still leaving room to question how it stacks up against market growth:
    • Supporters highlight that an 11.5% margin alongside high reported earnings quality backs the idea of resilient profitability through the recent periods.
    • Yet forecasts that earnings may grow about 10.34% a year and revenue about 5.2% a year, compared with the French market forecasts mentioned of 12% for earnings and 5.4% for revenue, mean the bullish narrative rests more on quality than on outperformance versus the broader market.

P/E of 16.5x vs DCF fair value gap

  • Robertet trades on a P/E of 16.5x, below the European Chemicals industry average of 17.5x and a peer average of 43.1x, while the current share price of €814 sits well under a DCF fair value of €1,395.78 and an analyst price target of €955.43.
  • What surprises some investors is how the valuation data creates a strong bullish tension between market pricing and model based estimates:
    • On one hand, a lower P/E than both industry and peer averages, together with the share price sitting about 41.7% below the DCF fair value, heavily supports the bullish argument that the market is pricing Robertet cautiously despite solid trailing earnings.
    • On the other hand, growth forecasts of about 10.34% a year for earnings and 5.2% a year for revenue provide a more moderate outlook, so the bullish case relies on the view that these growth rates justify a higher multiple than the current 16.5x.

At the same time, broader discussions around utilities modernization and grid resilience keep drawing attention to companies operating in this space, including Itron.

Approach 1: Itron Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting future cash flows and then discounting them back to today using a required return. It focuses on cash that could, in theory, be returned to shareholders over time.

For Itron, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow stands at about $378.7 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections suggest free cash flow of $302.8 million in 2026 and $315.4 million in 2027, with a projected $450 million by 2028. Simply Wall St then extends these estimates out to 2035 using gradually moderating growth assumptions.

When all those projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $167.28 per share. Compared with a recent share price around $97, this indicates a 42.0% discount, which suggests Itron is trading below this cash flow based estimate of value.

data.title: Post Holdings Faces Volume Pressure And Questions On Capital Returns
data.description:

  • Recent commentary highlights a heavy reliance on price increases rather than stronger demand to support revenue.
  • Market expectations currently point to stagnant revenues and below average capital returns, raising questions about the effectiveness of management's investment decisions.

For investors tracking NYSE:POST, the discussion around slowing unit volumes and dependence on pricing comes at a time when the stock trades at about $99.55 per share. The share price sits against a mixed performance record, with a 12.7% return over 3 years, a 29.7% return over 5 years, and a 13.5% decline over the past year. That combination puts the recent concerns about capital returns and growth opportunities into sharper focus.

Looking ahead, the key issues to watch are whether Post Holdings can stabilize unit sales and identify projects that lift capital returns from below average levels. For you as an investor, the balance between price driven revenue and real demand will likely be central to assessing the company’s longer term value creation potential.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Post Holdings by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Post Holdings.

3 things going right for Post Holdings that this headline doesn't cover.

Concerns about shrinking unit sales at Post Holdings and a heavier tilt toward price increases rather than volume growth point to a business model leaning more on pricing power than on consumer pull. For a packaged food company competing with players like General Mills, Kellogg and Kraft Heinz, that can be a fragile position if shoppers trade down to cheaper brands or private label. Flat revenue expectations and below average returns on capital also suggest that recent capital allocation, including acquisitions and internal projects, has not yet translated into stronger demand or higher productivity. For you as an investor, this combination raises questions about how effectively Post converts its spending into durable brands, distinctive products and efficient operations.

  • Persistent volume declines and below average capital returns challenge the idea that cost optimization and acquisitions alone can offset category pressure and competitive intensity.
  • The concern around weak investment returns may not fully reflect governance changes, share repurchases and any new capital allocation frameworks that could influence future decision making quality.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Post Holdings to help decide what it's worth to you.

  • 🎁 Forecast earnings growth and management’s willingness to repurchase shares signal an effort to improve per share outcomes and tighten capital allocation.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on whether Post can slow or reverse unit declines in cereal, pet food and other core categories while easing its dependence on price increases. data.title: Baidu (BIDU) Is Up 15.5% After Launching Apollo Go Driverless Service In Dubai
data.description:
• Baidu recently expanded its autonomous driving push, with Baidu AI Cloud unveiling its DuClaw zero-deployment service and Apollo Go surpassing 20 million fully driverless rides, alongside the March 31 launch of its first commercial driverless service outside China in Dubai with the Roads and Transport Authority.
  • This combination of large-scale driverless ride operations and an overseas commercial rollout underscores Baidu’s ambition to turn its AI and autonomous technologies into globally relevant, revenue-generating platforms.
  • We’ll now examine how Baidu’s international rollout of Apollo Go in Dubai could influence its broader investment narrative around AI-driven growth.

Baidu Investment Narrative Recap

To own Baidu, you need to believe its heavy AI and autonomous driving investments can eventually offset pressure on core online marketing and weak margins. The Dubai Apollo Go launch and DuClaw rollout support the AI platform story but do not immediately change the key near term catalyst, which remains clearer monetization of AI search and cloud, or the main risk, that these initiatives keep cash flow and margins under strain for longer.

Among recent announcements, Baidu’s new DuClaw zero deployment service is especially relevant here, because it highlights how Baidu is trying to productize its AI capabilities beyond China. If DuClaw and related AI cloud tools can build recurring, higher quality revenue, they could help balance the high fixed costs of Apollo Go’s expansion, including the Dubai rollout, and reduce dependence on China centric ad spending over time.
